variables.content: The proposition: For Fermi liquid parameters $F_l$ (or $F_l/m^{*}$) of the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) computed in square periodic simulation cells containing $N$ electrons, the leading systematic finite-size correction arising from long-range correlation effects scales asymptotically as $N^{\gamma}$ with exponent $\gamma=-1/4$ (i.e., a correction term proportional to $N^{-1/4}$), so that finite-$N$ results can be extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting to the form $F_l(N)=c+aN^{-1/4}$; this scaling is the same as that argued in the theoretical analyses of long-range-correlation finite-size effects (Holzmann et al.).

Using the reduced dimer Hamiltonian on a chain and the definition of the single-defect gap $\Delta$ as the energy difference between the uniform ground-sector and the sector with one flipped classical label, the additivity of excitation energies for two defects separated by more than two sites (energy $\approx2\Delta$) observed in perturbation theory and in finite-size calculations for $L\leq12$ is expected to persist in the thermodynamic limit $L\to\infty$ and therefore is not an artifact of small-system size or of periodic boundary conditions, because the underlying mechanism is locality of the first-order linear contribution and the short-range nature of quantum correlations.

Consider a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) in the thermodynamic limit defined by taking the particle number \\(N\\to\\infty\\) while holding the electron density fixed. Let the Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial many-electron wave function depend on finite-dimensional real parameter vectors \\(J\\) (the Jastrow-factor parameters controlling explicit two-body correlation terms, with polynomial and plane-wave expansions in interelectron distances cut off at the simulation-cell inscribed-circle radius) and \\(B\\) (the backflow-function parameters controlling polynomial backflow coordinate transformations). The proposition is: creating a single-particle excitation by adding or removing one electron at a definite wavevector \\(\\mathbf{k}\\) (changing the occupation by one at that \\(\\mathbf{k}\\) while preserving the thermodynamic-limit density) leaves the values of the optimal correlation-parameter vectors \\((J,B)\\) that minimize the ground-state energy per particle unchanged to leading order; equivalently, a single isolated quasiparticle excitation has a negligible effect on the optimal short- and medium-range two-body correlation functions and backflow transformations in the infinite 2D HEG.

Define the spin correlation in an excited eigenstate by $C_{ij}=\langle \Psi_{\mathrm{exc}}|\mathbf{S}_i\!\cdot\!\mathbf{S}_j|\Psi_{\mathrm{exc}}\rangle$, where $\mathbf{S}_i$ are spin-$\tfrac{1}{2}$ operators.  For $N=10$ the measured patterns $|C_{2,2+2r}|$ and $|C_{1,1+2r}|$ show non-monotonic dependence on $r$ and non-negligible magnitudes out to distances comparable to the cluster size.  The claim is: these finite-$N$ correlation patterns suggest that the corresponding excitations are not strongly localized (i.e., not supported only on a few neighboring spins) in the $N\to\infty$ limit.  The leap from irregular finite-$N$ correlation behavior to thermodynamic non-locality is uncertain because finite-size patterns can both over- and under-estimate delocalization in the infinite system.

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

For the one-dimensional spin-$3/2$ antiferromagnetic Heisenberg chain with Hamiltonian $\mathcal{H}_0=\sum_{j}\mathbf{S}_j\!\cdot\!\mathbf{S}_{j+1}+D\sum_j (S_j^z)^2$, let $E(L,M)$ denote the lowest eigenenergy of $\mathcal{H}_0$ in the sector with total magnetization $M$ for a finite even system size $L$. Define the finite-size excitation gap at fixed magnetization per site $m=M/L$ by $\Delta(L;m)\equiv E(L,M+1)+E(L,M-1)-2E(L,M)$. The proposition is: the thermodynamic-limit many-body excitation gap at fixed magnetization per site $m=1/2$ (i.e., whether $\lim_{L\to\infty}\Delta(L;m=1/2)$ is zero or nonzero) can be reliably inferred by extrapolating only the finite-size sequence $\Delta(L;m=1/2)$ computed by exact diagonalization for even system sizes $L\in\{6,8,10,12,14\}$, without requiring additional data at larger $L$ or independent numerical methods.

Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

The finite-system electron numbers used in the calculations—N = 26, 50, 74, 114 for paramagnetic HEGs and N = 29, 57, 101 for ferromagnetic HEGs—provide multiple system sizes at each density and spin polarization such that the computed values F_l^{s,a}/m^{*}(N) display the onset of finite-size behavior consistent with long-range-correlation-dominated scaling (for example an N^{-1/4} trend), and therefore these system sizes were treated as sufficient for attempting an extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit using the assumed N^{-1/4} dependence.

Assuming the Perdew–Burke–Wang (PBW) gradient correction (with coupling-constant average removed) is representative of gradient-corrected HEG correlation-hole models that one would apply in the short-range replacement (the SRCH short-range replacement being the construction that uses a local-HEG model for the short-range part of the correlation hole multiplied by a short-range cut-off function $f(u)$), the observed facts that PBW leaves the on-top ($u=0$) hole unchanged and is positive at small $u$ imply that gradient corrections of the PBW type do not remedy the principal LDA deficiency in the on-top hole and therefore provide no net benefit when combined with the SRCH short-range replacement as formulated in this work.

For the spin-$2$ isotropic Heisenberg chain $H=J\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl(S_i^xS_{i+1}^x+S_i^yS_{i+1}^y+S_i^zS_{i+1}^z\bigr)$, the RSRG-computed thermodynamic-limit excitation gap $G^{RG}$ is assumed to be sufficiently accurate and unbiased that qualitative agreement between $G^{RG}$ and an independently extrapolated exact-diagonalization estimate $G^{ED}(\infty)$ provides meaningful support for the numerical value of the true gap $G(\infty)$.
